content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


May 8, 1995


Mr. Richard Mayer
Board Member, Division III
Central Basin Municipal Water District
1810 So. Fair Oaks, Suite 300
So. Pasadena, California 91030

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-147

Dear Mr. Mayer:

You have asked for advice concerning the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May you respond to postcards and letters which you received
in your capacity as Director of the Central Basin Municipal Water
District at district expense?

CONCLUSION

Yes. It would not constitute a prohibited mass mailing under
the Act if you respond with a signed letter sent at district
expense to the postcards and letters you received.

FACTS

You are an elected director of the Central Basin Municipal
Water District governing board. In your capacity as a director,
you received approximately 1,200 postcards and letters opposing
the imposition by the district of a standby charge. You want to
respond to these postcards and letters with a signed letter which
will be sent at district expense.


___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-95-147
Page 2


ANALYSIS

Section 89001 of the Act provides that no newsletter or other
mass mailing shall be sent at public expense. Regulation 18901(a)
states that a mass mailing is prohibited if all of the following
criteria are met:

(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to
the recipient at his or her residence, place of employ-
ment or business, or post office box. For purposes of
this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the
recipient must be a tangible item, such as a videotape,
record, or button, or a written document.

(2) The item sent either:

(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with
the agency which produces or sends the mailing, or

(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or
other reference to an elected officer affiliated with
the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, co-
ordination, or concert with the elected officer.

(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid
for with public moneys; or

(B) Costs of design, production, and printing
exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and the
design, production, or printing is done with the intent
of sending the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.

(4) More than two hundred substantially similar
items are sent, in a single calendar month, excluding
any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and
any item described in subdivision (b).

(Emphasis added.)

An "unsolicited request" is defined in Regulation
18901(c)(4)(A) as follows:

A written or oral communication (including a
petition) which specifically requests a response and
which is not requested or induced by the recipient
elected officer or by any third person acting at his or
her behest. However, an unsolicited oral or written
communication (including a petition) which contains no
specific request for a response, will be deemed to
constitute an unsolicited request for a single written
response.

File No. A-95-147
Page 3


The above regulation excludes from the definition of a "mass
mailing" items sent in response to an unsolicited request.
Constituents sent you postcards and letters expressing their
opposition to the district's imposition of a charge. You did not
solicit or request the communications. The regulation
specifically states that such unsolicited postcards or letters
which contain no specific request for a response shall be deemed
to constitute an "unsolicited request for a single written
response." Therefore, you may respond to these postcards and
letters with a signed letter sent out at district expense. Such a
mailing would not be considered a prohibited mass mailing at
public expense under the Act.

I trust this answers your question. For your information,
enclosed is a copy of Regulation 18901 and a Commission fact sheet
on mass mailings. If you have any further questions regarding
this matter, please contact me at 916/322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

Hyla P. Wagner

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Counsel, Legal Division


Enclosures

in your capacity as Director of the Central Basin Municipal Water
District at district expense?

CONCLUSION: Yes.
It would not constitute a prohibited mass mailing under
the Act if you respond with a signed letter sent at district
expense to the postcards and letters you received.
